explanation.```\nI am the main thread\nI am Thread 3: Correct. Thread execution is non-deterministic — the JVM and OS scheduler may run threads in any order. An output is valid as long as all 4 lines appear exactly once.
explanation.```\nI am the main thread\nI am Thread 1: Correct. Thread execution is non-deterministic — the JVM and OS scheduler may run threads in any order. An output is valid as long as all 4 lines appear exactly once.
explanation.```\nI am Thread 1\nI am Thread 2\nI am: Correct. Thread execution is non-deterministic — the JVM and OS scheduler may run threads in any order. An output is valid as long as all 4 lines appear exactly once.
Correct. Thread execution is non-deterministic — the JVM and OS scheduler may run threads in any order. An output is valid as long as all 4 lines appear exactly once.
Incorrect. The main thread also prints a statement. All 4 lines must appear exactly once in any order.

questions.source_reference: W13 Concurrency – Threads, non-deterministic execution, thread scheduling

options: The program will compile but not print anything.

explanation.`HELLO WORLD!`: Correct. The pipeline concatenates "Hello" and " World", converts the result to uppercase via thenApply(String::toUpperCase) to produce "HELLO WORLD", then appends "!" in the final thenCompose step.
explanation.`Hello World!`: Incorrect. The string is converted to uppercase before the exclamation mark is appended, so the result cannot be mixed-case.
explanation.`Hello World`: Incorrect. The exclamation mark is added in the final thenCompose step, and the string is also converted to uppercase earlier in the chain.
Incorrect. The program compiles successfully — there are no syntax errors in the code.
explanation.The program will compile but not print a: Incorrect. The thenAccept(System.out::println) call registers a consumer that prints the final result.

questions.source_reference: W13 Concurrency – CompletableFuture, thenApply(), thenCompose(), thenAccept()

options: Three tasks will be executed, but only two threads will be used by the `executor`.
Only two tasks will be executed, as there are two threads in the pool.
The tasks may be executed in any order, depending on thread scheduling.
The program will wait indefinitely for a thread to become available, and the third task will never execute.
correct_answer: Three tasks will be executed, but only two threads will be used by the `executor`.
The tasks may be executed in any order, depending on thread scheduling.

explanation.Three tasks will be executed, but only t: Correct. The fixed thread pool size is 2, so two tasks run immediately while the third waits in the queue for a thread to become free. All three tasks are eventually executed.
explanation.Only two tasks will be executed, as ther: Incorrect. The pool size determines the number of concurrent threads, not the total number of tasks executed. All three submitted tasks will run.
explanation.The tasks may be executed in any order,: Correct. The order in which tasks are picked up and executed by the two threads is non-deterministic and depends on the executor's internal scheduling.
explanation.The program will wait indefinitely for a: Incorrect. Once either of the two threads finishes its current task, it picks up the waiting third task. The executor does not block indefinitely.

learning_objectives: Understand that thread execution order is non-deterministic and predict all valid outputs of a multithreaded program
Trace a CompletableFuture pipeline through thenApply() and thenCompose() to determine the final output
Distinguish between thread pool size and total task count in a fixed-size ExecutorService, and understand non-deterministic task ordering
